skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use inference.sh from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to discover, run, and monitor cloud AI apps for media, search, LLM, and automation tasks.
skill.summary: Run cloud AI apps through the inference.sh CLI from supported agent tools.
skill.author: tul-sh

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly detected many command, network, credential, and filesystem patterns, but most are Markdown examples for the intended inference.sh CLI workflow. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill still warrants a medium warning because it recommends a pipe-to-shell installer, can run networked cloud AI jobs, and documents API key use.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Recommendation
medium_findings.description: The install instructions pipe a remote script directly into the shell. This can execute changed remote content before local review, although the same file also documents checksum verification and a manual install path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present in multiple installation examples. Confidence is high for the risk pattern, but no malicious intent is shown because a manual verified install is also documented.

medium_findings.title: Broad Networked CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access to any infsh command and demonstrates remote AI runs, web search, and Twitter posting. This is core functionality, but it can send user prompts and create external account actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command scope and examples clearly show networked remote execution. The use is legitimate for this skill, but the permission is broad and user data can leave the local environment.

medium_findings.title: API Key and Local Credential Handling
medium_findings.description: The documentation tells users to authenticate and optionally set INFSH_API_KEY. Secrets are expected for operation, so users must avoid exposing keys in prompts, logs, and shared files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly mention local credential storage and an API key environment variable. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates those credentials.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Mistaken for Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are Markdown examples of infsh commands, not Ruby backticks or executable skill code. They are expected for a CLI reference skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are fenced code blocks or command tables. They document intended CLI usage and do not create a command injection path by themselves.

low_findings.title: Checksum Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged cryptography references, but the reviewed content describes SHA-256 checksum verification for downloaded binaries. No weak algorithm use was found in these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md lines explicitly refer to SHA-256 verification. The other static locations are model names and app identifiers, not cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Expected Local File Writes for CLI Workflow
low_findings.description: The filesystem detections involve installing a binary under a user local bin path, shell completions, sample input files, and saved task output. These are normal CLI behaviors but should remain user-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file writes are explicit documentation examples, not hidden persistence. They still touch local paths and generated files, so the evidence remains a low concern.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Product and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, installer, image hosting, and app output examples. I did not find evidence of covert exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the documented inference.sh service and examples. The risk is ordinary network dependency, not suspicious data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer pattern downloads a script over the network and executes it immediately. Users should prefer the documented manual download and checksum verification path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in install examples. It is a common convenience install pattern, so this is risky but not proof of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad infsh Command Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash(infsh *), which can run any supported inference.sh CLI subcommand. This includes cloud jobs, deployment commands, account queries, and task retrieval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist pattern is explicit and broad within the infsh namespace. It is constrained to one CLI, but that CLI controls remote services.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Environment Variable
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation uses INFSH_API_KEY for CI and scripts. This is normal authentication support, but mishandling can expose account credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential variable is documented clearly. I did not find a code path that reads and transmits it outside the intended CLI authentication flow.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps from Your Agent
content.value_statement: Teams need quick access to many AI models without managing GPUs or separate SDKs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through inference.sh CLI workflows for cloud AI tasks.
actual_capabilities: Installs and authenticates the inference.sh CLI when users choose to follow setup instructions.
Lists, searches, filters, and inspects available inference.sh apps by category or query.
Runs cloud AI apps with file or inline inputs through infsh app run commands.
Tracks long-running tasks and retrieves saved results through infsh task commands.
Provides examples for image, video, LLM, search, 3D, audio, and Twitter workflows.
Documents development commands for initializing, testing, deploying, and pulling inference.sh apps.
limitations: Requires an installed infsh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Cloud runs may consume credits and send prompts or files to external services.
App availability, model names, pricing, and output formats can change outside this skill.
Twitter and deployment commands can modify external accounts when users approve them.

use_cases.title: Prototype multimodal AI features
use_cases.description: Find and run image, video, speech, search, and LLM apps from one CLI-driven workflow.

use_cases.title: Operate cloud inference tasks
use_cases.description: Submit long-running jobs, poll task status, and save outputs without managing GPU infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Explore model options quickly
use_cases.description: Search the app catalog, inspect schemas, and compare providers before integrating a model.

prompt_templates.title: List available apps
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to list available inference.sh apps for image generation and summarize the most relevant choices.

prompt_templates.title: Generate media with a known app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to run the FLUX image app with this prompt: a clean product photo of a red backpack.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a long-running video job
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to create a sample input for a Veo video app, run it without waiting, and show how to check the task later.

prompt_templates.title: Compare and select a model workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to search for web research and LLM apps, compare likely inputs and outputs, then recommend a workflow for market analysis.

output_examples.input: Search for image generation apps and recommend one for quick concept art.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant image apps, highlights fast and high-quality options, and recommends a command to inspect the chosen app schema.

output_examples.input: Run a video generation task without waiting and explain how to retrieve it.
output_examples.output: The assistant submits the task, records the task identifier shown by the CLI, and gives the follow-up command for status and results.

output_examples.input: Check authentication and explain how to use an API key in CI.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies the current user, explains INFSH_API_KEY usage, and warns not to expose the key in shared logs.

best_practices: Review app schemas with infsh app get before running jobs that use files, secrets, or paid models.
Prefer the manual verified installer path when operating in sensitive environments.
Confirm external actions such as posting, deploying, or spending credits before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys, session tokens, or private files into prompts or shared transcripts.
Do not run Twitter, deployment, or account-changing commands without explicit user intent.
Do not assume generated URLs or task outputs are private unless the service documentation confirms it.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps supported agents use the inference.sh CLI to discover, run, and monitor cloud AI apps.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. Most workflows require the infsh CLI and an authenticated account or API key.

faq.question: Can it run image and video models?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include examples for FLUX image apps and Veo video apps.

faq.question: Can it access external services?
faq.answer: Yes. It runs cloud apps through inference.sh and may call third-party model or search providers.

faq.question: Is the installer safe for restricted environments?
faq.answer: Use caution. The quick installer pipes a remote script to the shell, and the manual verified path is safer.

faq.question: Can it change external accounts?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include Twitter actions and deployment commands, so users should approve those actions explicitly.
